title: Ski Cooper

infobox.value: [Eagle](/wiki/Eagle_County,_Colorado "Eagle County, Colorado") / [Lake](/wiki/Lake_County,_Colorado "Lake County, Colorado") counties, [Colorado](/wiki/Colorado "Colorado"), [USA](/wiki/United_States "United States")
470 acres (1.9 km2) lift served  2,400 acres (9.7 km2) snowcat served

is an [alpine](/wiki/Alpine_skiing "Alpine skiing") [ski resort](/wiki/Ski_resort "Ski resort") in [Colorado](/wiki/Colorado "Colorado"), one of the oldest in the state. Opened as **Cooper Hill Ski Area** in 1942, the ski area served as the training site for the [10th Mountain Division](/wiki/10th_Mountain_Division "10th Mountain Division"), based at nearby [Camp Hale](/wiki/Camp_Hale "Camp Hale") during [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II").[[1]](#cite_note-hist-1)

Blevins, Jason. ["Ski Cooper's expanded expert terrain, financial vibrancy reveal model for nonprofit ski area management"](https://coloradosun.com/2020/02/17/ski-cooper-new-expert-terrain-open/). *The Colorado Sun*.

**Ski Cooper** is an [alpine](/wiki/Alpine_skiing "Alpine skiing") [ski resort](/wiki/Ski_resort "Ski resort") in [Colorado](/wiki/Colorado "Colorado"), one of the oldest in the state. Opened as **Cooper Hill Ski Area** in 1942, the ski area served as the training site for the [10th Mountain Division](/wiki/10th_Mountain_Division "10th Mountain Division"), based at nearby [Camp Hale](/wiki/Camp_Hale "Camp Hale") during [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II").[[1]](#cite_note-hist-1)

The resort is owned by [Lake County](/wiki/Lake_County,_Colorado "Lake County, Colorado") and managed by Cooper Hill Ski Area, a [501(c)4](/wiki/501(c)4 "501(c)4") nonprofit with a volunteer board. Dan Torsell has served as the general manager since 2012.[[2]](#cite_note-sun-2)

## Location

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ski_Cooper&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Location")]

Ski Cooper is located at [Tennessee Pass](/wiki/Tennessee_Pass_(Colorado) "Tennessee Pass (Colorado)"), at the dividing line between the [San Isabel National Forest](/wiki/San_Isabel_National_Forest "San Isabel National Forest")[[3]](#cite_note-3) and the [White River National Forest](/wiki/White_River_National_Forest "White River National Forest"), near [Leadville](/wiki/Leadville "Leadville").

## Description

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ski_Cooper&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Description")]

Ski Cooper markets itself as a family resort because of its affordability and accessibility. It is among the most affordable ski areas in the state, second to only [Howelsen Hill](/wiki/Howelsen_Hill "Howelsen Hill") in [Steamboat Springs](/wiki/Steamboat_Springs,_Colorado "Steamboat Springs, Colorado").[[4]](#cite_note-4)

The resort has 64 runs of varying difficulty serviced by two [chairlifts](/wiki/Chairlift "Chairlift") (one Hall/CTEC double and one Poma triple), one Doppelmayr platter lift, one Leitner t-bar, and one Magic Carpet conveyor lift. It has ample beginner and intermediate terrain, along with tree and glade skiing, ungroomed and mogul terrain, all with exclusively natural snow.[[5]](#cite_note-5) Dining options at the resort include a cafeteria, an Irish pub, a Taproom, and a restaurant in a mountaintop yurt. Equipment rental and a retail store are also available on the property.

Ski Cooper is home to the Chicago Ridge Snowcat Tours which provides access to backcountry glades and powder bowls. The resort has an active alpine race department, and has also been known to host Ski & Snowboard Cross NorAm level races.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ski_Cooper&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: History")]

The Cooper Hill Ski Area, as it was then known, was founded in 1942. That year, the [US Army](/wiki/US_Army "US Army") selected the area to serve as the 10th Mountain Division training site because of its proximity to the [Pando](/wiki/Pando,_Colorado "Pando, Colorado") rail station.[[1]](#cite_note-hist-1) Ski Cooper opened to the public as a ski resort in the postwar period and continues to host a [Memorial Day](/wiki/Memorial_Day "Memorial Day") celebration for the World War II veterans of the 10th Mountain Division.

In January 2020, the resort expanded with the opening of the Tennessee Creek Basin Area.[[6]](#cite_note-6)
